name: Tel Dan Stele
description: An Aramaic inscription that likely contains the earliest extra-biblical reference to the 'House of David'.
biblicalConnections: Frequently discussed in connection with Davidic dynasty references in Kings and Samuel.
significance: Important for the history of Israel, Aram-Damascus, and debates over dynastic language.
caution: The inscription is genuine, but the reconstruction and interpretation of damaged lines remain debated.

name: Mesha Stele
description: A Moabite inscription describing King Mesha's achievements and conflict with Israel.
biblicalConnections: Often discussed alongside the Moabite conflict narrative in Kings.
significance: A major Iron Age West Semitic inscription and an important external witness to Moab and Israel.
caution: The stele is genuine, but exact readings of a few damaged areas still draw scholarly discussion.

name: Pilate Stone
description: A Latin inscription naming Pontius Pilate and documenting Roman administration in Judea.
biblicalConnections: A key artifact for the historical setting of the Passion narratives.
significance: Confirms the historical name and title of Pilate as prefect in Judea.
caution: The inscription is clear, but it does not by itself explain Pilate's actions in the Gospels.

name: Dead Sea Scrolls
currentLocation: Shrine of the Book, Israel Museum
description: A large collection of ancient Jewish manuscripts discovered in caves near Qumran.
biblicalConnections: Important for the textual history of the Hebrew Bible and Second Temple Judaism.
significance: The scrolls are foundational evidence for textual transmission and Jewish diversity in the late Second Temple period.
caution: The discovery is secure, but the social identity of the Qumran community remains debated.

name: Siloam Inscription
description: A Hebrew inscription commemorating the completion of the Siloam Tunnel.
biblicalConnections: Often discussed in relation to Hezekiah's water works in Jerusalem.
significance: One of the clearest surviving Hebrew construction inscriptions from ancient Judah.
caution: The inscription is genuine, but the exact royal attribution is inferred from context rather than named in the text.

name: Cyrus Cylinder
description: A clay cylinder recording Cyrus's policy after the conquest of Babylon.
biblicalConnections: Frequently compared with the Persian return tradition in Ezra.
significance: An important witness to Persian imperial ideology and the broader restoration context.
caution: The cylinder is genuine, but it is not a direct copy of the biblical decree.

name: Taylor Prism
description: A prism inscribed with Sennacherib's annals, including the campaign against Judah.
biblicalConnections: Important for comparing Assyrian royal rhetoric with the biblical account of the siege of Jerusalem.
significance: A major Assyrian source for imperial campaigns and propaganda.
caution: The prism is genuine, but royal inscriptions are propagandistic and need careful comparison with other sources.

name: Black Obelisk
description: A Neo-Assyrian monument showing tribute scenes, including a figure often identified as Jehu.
biblicalConnections: Frequently linked to the political world of Jehu and the Omride dynasty.
significance: One of the most famous visual sources for biblical-era Near Eastern diplomacy.
caution: The Jehu identification is widely accepted, but the monument still functions as royal ideology, not neutral reportage.

name: Merneptah Stele
description: A commemorative stele that includes the earliest widely accepted extra-biblical reference to 'Israel'.
biblicalConnections: Important for the late Bronze Age background of Israel's emergence in Canaan.
significance: A key Egyptian witness to the historical memory of Israel in the late 13th century BCE.
caution: The reading is broad scholarly consensus, but the term 'Israel' reflects an Egyptian geopolitical label rather than a biblical narrative.

name: Lachish Reliefs
description: A sequence of reliefs depicting Sennacherib's siege of Lachish.
biblicalConnections: Provides visual evidence for the Assyrian siege context behind Judah's crisis.
significance: One of the strongest archaeological depictions of a biblical-era military event.
caution: The reliefs are explicit about the siege, but they still represent Assyrian royal perspective.

name: Ketef Hinnom Silver Scrolls
description: Two tiny silver scrolls inscribed with a form of the priestly blessing.
biblicalConnections: An important witness to early biblical text tradition and blessing language.
significance: Among the oldest known Hebrew textual witnesses linked to the Bible.
caution: The texts are secure, but they are fragmentary and require careful epigraphic reconstruction.

name: James Ossuary
description: An ossuary with an inscription that has been proposed as referring to James, the brother of Jesus.
biblicalConnections: Often discussed in relation to the New Testament figure of James.
significance: Famous because of the inscription and the controversy around provenance and reading.
caution: Provenance, authenticity, and inscription interpretation are disputed; BHF treats this as a debated item rather than a settled identification.
